mlsynth 0.1 documentation

Get started

  • About mlsynth
  • A practitioner’s decision tree
  • Replications
    • FDID — Forward Difference-in-Differences (Li 2024)
    • TSSC — Two-Step Synthetic Control (Li & Shankar 2024)
    • VanillaSC — Standard Synthetic Control (ADH 2010/2015; Abadie-Gardeazabal 2003)
    • Ridge ASCM — Augmented Synthetic Control (Ben-Michael, Feller & Rothstein 2021)
    • SparseSC — L1 Predictor Selection for Synthetic Controls (Vives-i-Bastida 2022)
    • NSC — Nonlinear Synthetic Control (Tian 2023)
    • SDID — Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (Arkhangelsky et al. 2021)
    • MCNNM — Matrix Completion with Nuclear-Norm Minimization (Athey et al. 2021)
    • SpSyDiD — Spatial Synthetic-DiD (Serenini & Masek 2024)
    • SEQ_SDID — Sequential Synthetic DiD (Arkhangelsky & Samkov 2025)
    • ClusterSC — Synthetic Control with Donor Selection (Rho et al. 2025)
    • LEXSCM — Synthetic Experimental Design (Abadie-Zhao 2026; Vives-i-Bastida 2022)
    • SCMO — Synthetic Control with Multiple Outcomes (Tian et al. 2026; Sun et al. 2025)
    • RESCM — SCM-relaxation (Liao, Shi & Zheng 2026)
    • LINF / L1LINF — L-infinity-norm SC (Wang, Xing & Ye 2025)
    • GEOLIFT — Meta’s GeoLift walkthrough (augsynth cross-validation)
    • ROLLDID — Lee & Wooldridge rolling-transformation DiD
    • PPSCM — augsynth multisynth (Paglayan collective bargaining)
    • SNN — deshen24/syntheticNN (Prop 99)
    • SYNDES — Doudchenko et al. (2021) BLS Monte Carlo
    • SPCD — Lu et al. (2022) Prop 99 design study
    • SPOTSYNTH — O’Riordan & Gilligan-Lee (2025) spillover detection
    • MAREX — Abadie & Zhao (2026) Walmart design (independent validator)
    • DSC — Distributional Synthetic Controls on Dube (2019)
    • MicroSynth — panel method vs the R microsynth (Seattle DMI)
  • Benchmarks
  • References

Observational: canonical workhorses

  • Vanilla Synthetic Control (VanillaSC)
  • Two-Step Synthetic Control
  • Forward Difference-in-Differences (FDID)

Observational: decomposition-first

  • Synthetic Business Cycle (SBC)
  • Harmonic Synthetic Control (HSC)

Observational: generalised estimand / treatment / unit

  • Synthetic Control with Multiple Outcomes (SCMO)
  • Continuous-Treatment Synthetic Control (CTSC)
  • Distributional Synthetic Control (DSC)
  • Synthetic Interventions (SI)
  • MicroSynth (User-Level Balancing SC)

Observational: convex-hull relaxation

  • Imperfect Synthetic Controls (ISCM)
  • Nonlinear Synthetic Control (NSC)

Observational: No Donors

  • Synthetic Historical Control (SHC)

Observational: high-dimensional donors

  • Bayesian Synthetic Control with a Soft Simplex Constraint (BVS-SS)
  • Cluster Synthetic Controls (CLUSTERSC)
  • Multi-Level Synthetic Control (mlSC)
  • Forward-Selected Synthetic Control (FSCM)
  • Matching and Synthetic Control (MASC)
  • Multivariate Square-root Lasso Synthetic Control (MSQRT)
  • Sparse Synthetic Control (SparseSC)
  • Panel Data Approach (PDA)
  • Relaxed / Penalized Synthetic Control (RESCM)

Observational: time-aware and factor models

  • Time-Aware Synthetic Control (TASC)
  • Factor Model Approach (FMA)
  • Dynamic Synthetic Control for Auto-Regressive processes (DSCAR)

Observational: staggered adoption

  • Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID)
  • Sequential Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (Sequential SDiD)
  • Partially Pooled SCM (PPSCM)
  • Staggered Synthetic Control (SSC)

Observational: spillover-aware

  • Spatial Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SpSyDiD)
  • Spillover-Aware Synthetic Control (SPILLSYNTH)
  • Spillover-Detecting Synthetic Control (SPOTSYNTH)

Observational: missing data

  • Matrix Completion with Nuclear Norm Minimization (MCNNM)
  • Synthetic Nearest Neighbors / Causal Matrix Completion (SNN)
  • Robust Matrix estimation with Side Information (RMSI)

Observational: endogenous treatment

  • Synthetic IV
  • Proximal Inference Synthetic Control (PROXIMAL)

Experimental design

  • Lexicographic Synthetic Control (LEXSCM)
  • Synthetic Controls for Experimental Design (MAREX)
  • Synthetic Design (SYNDES)
  • Parallel-Trends Supergeo Design (PANGEO)
  • Synthetic Principal Component Design (SPCD)
  • Modified Unbiased Synthetic Control
  • Rolling-Transformation DiD (ROLLDID)

Utilities and internals

  • Helper Utilities

MULTICELLGEOLIFT — multi-cell GeoLift analysis

Contents

  • When to use
  • Data model
  • Notation
  • Assumptions
  • Method
  • Result
  • Reading the results — per-cell plots and the comparison
  • Not voodoo — one cell is the single-cell case
  • Verification
  • Core API
    • MULTICELLGEOLIFT
      • MULTICELLGEOLIFT.fit()
    • MultiCellGeoLiftConfig
      • MultiCellGeoLiftConfig.model_config

MULTICELLGEOLIFT — multi-cell GeoLift analysis#

When to use#

A multi-cell geo experiment runs several treatments at once — different channels, budgets, or creative strategies — each on its own group of geos (“cells” \(A, B, \dots\)), all measured against a shared pool of control geos over the same window. Use MULTICELLGEOLIFT to measure each cell’s incremental effect and to compare the cells. It is the analysis analogue of GeoLift’s GeoLiftMultiCell (single-cell measurement is GeoLift Market Selection (GEOLIFT)).

Data model#

A unit-level cell-membership column plus a treatment-window indicator:

  • cell_column_name — each geo’s cell label ("A", "B", …); blank / NaN (or an explicit control_label) marks a control geo. The label is a property of the geo, so it is constant over that geo’s rows.

  • post_col — the (shared) 0/1 post-treatment window.

import pandas as pd
from mlsynth import MULTICELLGEOLIFT

url = ("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jgreathouse9/mlsynth/"
       "refs/heads/main/basedata/geolift_test_data.csv")
df = pd.read_csv(url)                                   # GeoLift_Test: 40 mkts x 105 days
dates = sorted(df["date"].unique())
df["post"] = df["date"].isin(dates[90:]).astype(int)   # last 15 days = treatment window

#   cell A -> social-media markets, cell B -> paid-search markets, blank = control
cell = {"chicago": "A", "portland": "A", "atlanta": "B", "boston": "B"}
df["cell"] = df["location"].map(cell).fillna("")        # blank = shared control pool

res = MULTICELLGEOLIFT({
    "df": df, "outcome": "Y", "unitid": "location", "time": "date",
    "cell_column_name": "cell",   # "A"/"B"/... ; blank = control
    "post_col": "post",
    "fixed_effects": True,         # augsynth/GeoLift default
}).fit()

res.cells["A"].effects.att        # cell A's ATT (per unit)
res.cells["A"].inference.p_value  # cell A's conformal p
res.comparison                    # pairwise cross-cell rows
res.winner                        # the cell that wins every comparison, or None

Notation#

This estimator is a thin multi-cell wrapper, so its symbols are GEOLIFT’s (GeoLift Market Selection (GEOLIFT)) applied once per cell. There are \(N\) markets \(\mathcal{N} \coloneqq \{1, \dots, N\}\) over periods \(t \in \mathcal{T} \coloneqq \{1, \dots, T\}\), with the intervention taking effect after \(T_0\), splitting \(\mathcal{T}\) into the pre-period \(\mathcal{T}_1 \coloneqq \{t \in \mathcal{T} : t \le T_0\}\) and the post-period \(\mathcal{T}_2 \coloneqq \{t \in \mathcal{T} : t > T_0\}\). The outcome of market \(j\) at time \(t\) is \(y_{jt}\), with market series \(\mathbf{y}_j \in \mathbb{R}^{T}\).

Each cell \(c \in \{A, B, \dots\}\) is a treated region \(\mathcal{S}_c \subseteq \mathcal{N}\) — a GEOLIFT design in its own right, so \(\mathcal{S}_c\) plays the canonical treated role through its aggregate series \(\mathbf{y}^{\mathcal{S}_c}\) (cf. GEOLIFT’s treated set \(\mathcal{S}\)). The shared control pool is every market in no cell, \(\mathcal{N}_0 \coloneqq \mathcal{N} \setminus \bigcup_c \mathcal{S}_c\) with cardinality \(N_0\), giving the donor matrix \(\mathbf{Y}_0 \coloneqq [\mathbf{y}_j]_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0}\). Cell \(c\)’s donor pool excludes the other cells’ markets, \(\mathcal{N} \setminus \bigl(\mathcal{S}_c \cup \bigcup_{c' \neq c} \mathcal{S}_{c'}\bigr) = \mathcal{N}_0\). The per-period effect for cell \(c\) is \(\tau_t \coloneqq y^{\mathcal{S}_c}_t - \widehat{y}^{\mathcal{S}_c}_t\) and its ATT is \(\widehat{\tau} \coloneqq |\mathcal{T}_2|^{-1} \sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}_2} \tau_t\), as in GeoLift Market Selection (GEOLIFT).

Assumptions#

Each cell inherits GEOLIFT’s per-cell identifying assumptions; the multi-cell wrapper adds one cross-cell condition.

  1. Pre-period synthesizability. Each cell’s aggregate \(\mathbf{y}^{\mathcal{S}_c}\) lies in (or near) the span / convex hull of the control pool over \(\mathcal{T}_1\) (GEOLIFT’s scaled-L2 imbalance \(\kappa(\mathcal{S}_c)\) certifies it; see GeoLift Market Selection (GEOLIFT)).

    Remark. This is the prerequisite for a credible per-cell counterfactual, inspected cell by cell exactly as in the single-cell case.

  2. Exchangeability under the null. The conformal test treats each cell’s residual path as exchangeable under \(H_0\) of no effect.

    Remark. The same all-period refit GEOLIFT uses to deliver this runs once per cell, so each cell’s conformal p-value is read on the single-cell terms of GeoLift Market Selection (GEOLIFT).

  3. Placebo-window stationarity. Pre-period dynamics resemble the experiment window, so the design transports — the usual SC stability assumption, applied per cell.

    Remark. A regime change between \(\mathcal{T}_1\) and \(\mathcal{T}_2\) breaks the counterfactual for that cell even with a good pre-fit.

  4. Cross-cell non-contamination. The other cells’ markets are excluded from a cell’s donor pool: cell \(c\)’s synthetic control combines control geos only, never any \(\mathcal{S}_{c'}\) for \(c' \neq c\).

    Remark. Other cells are treated (with a different treatment) and so contaminated; admitting them as donors would bias the counterfactual. This is GeoLift’s filter(!location %in% other_cells).

Method#

Per cell. Each cell is measured against the shared control pool with the fixed-effect Augmented SCM + conformal inference of GeoLift Market Selection (GEOLIFT) — crucially, the other cells’ geos are excluded from the donor pool, because they are treated (with a different treatment) and so contaminated (GeoLift’s filter(!location %in% other_cells)). So cell \(A\)’s synthetic control is a combination of control geos only, never cell \(B\)’s.

Cross-cell winner. For each pair, a cell wins when its ATT confidence interval lies strictly above the other’s (GeoLift’s non-overlapping-CI rule; the ATT interval is the per-period conformal band averaged). The overall winner wins every pairwise comparison, else None. This is deliberately conservative: measuring each cell cleanly is well-powered, but separating two cells needs the difference to clear both intervals — so overlapping CIs (no declared winner) is common and correct, not a failure.

Result#

MULTICELLGEOLIFT.fit returns a MultiCellResults (a DesignResult): cells maps each label to its EffectResult, comparison is the pairwise table, winner the overall winner, and report is the representative (winner / largest) cell.

Reading the results — per-cell plots and the comparison#

Each cell’s report is a full GeoLift EffectResult, so every single-cell view (observed vs synthetic, the gap with its conformal band, the donor weights — see GeoLift Market Selection (GEOLIFT)) works per cell. plot_multicell stacks the observed-vs-synthetic panels, one row per cell:

import pandas as pd
from mlsynth import MULTICELLGEOLIFT
from mlsynth.utils.geolift_helpers.multicell.plotter import plot_multicell

url = ("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jgreathouse9/mlsynth/"
       "refs/heads/main/basedata/geolift_test_data.csv")
df = pd.read_csv(url)                                   # GeoLift_Test: 40 mkts x 105 days
dates = sorted(df["date"].unique())
df["post"] = df["date"].isin(dates[90:]).astype(int)   # last 15 days = treatment window
cell = {"chicago": "A", "portland": "A", "atlanta": "B", "boston": "B"}
df["cell"] = df["location"].map(cell).fillna("")        # blank = shared control pool

res = MULTICELLGEOLIFT({
    "df": df, "outcome": "Y", "unitid": "location", "time": "date",
    "cell_column_name": "cell", "post_col": "post", "fixed_effects": True,
}).fit()

plot_multicell(res, show=True)                        # one panel per cell

# per-cell numbers and views
res.cells["A"].effects.att, res.cells["A"].inference.p_value
res.cells["A"].time_series.estimated_gap              # cell A's gap path
res.cells["A"].weights.donor_weights                  # cell A's controls

# cross-cell comparison and the overall winner
res.comparison        # [{cell_a, cell_b, att_a, att_b, att_diff, winner}, ...]
res.winner            # the cell that wins every pairwise comparison, or None

A winner of None is the honest, common outcome: each cell is measured well, but declaring one better needs its ATT interval to clear the other’s (GeoLift’s non-overlapping-CI rule), which a single test rarely supports.

Not voodoo — one cell is the single-cell case#

Multi-cell strictly generalizes single-cell: with one cell, every other unit is a control (no other cells to exclude), so MULTICELLGEOLIFT makes the same fit as the single-cell GeoLift Market Selection (GEOLIFT) realize — same treated set, same donor pool, hence the same ATT, conformal p, and weights (pinned in test_multicell.py):

import pandas as pd
from mlsynth import MULTICELLGEOLIFT

url = ("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jgreathouse9/mlsynth/"
       "refs/heads/main/basedata/geolift_test_data.csv")
df = pd.read_csv(url)                                   # GeoLift_Test: 40 mkts x 105 days
dates = sorted(df["date"].unique())
df["post"] = df["date"].isin(dates[90:]).astype(int)   # last 15 days = treatment window

# one cell A only; every other geo is a control (no other cells to exclude)
df["cell"] = df["location"].map({"chicago": "A", "portland": "A"}).fillna("")

res = MULTICELLGEOLIFT({
    "df": df, "outcome": "Y", "unitid": "location", "time": "date",
    "cell_column_name": "cell", "post_col": "post", "fixed_effects": True,
}).fit()

# one cell A, everyone else control  ==  single-cell GEOLIFT on {chicago, portland}
res.cells["A"].effects.att        # 156.805165  (identical to the realize ATT)
res.cells["A"].inference.p_value  # 0.006        (identical)
res.winner                        # None — nothing to compare against

Verification#

Cross-validated against augsynth (the engine GeoLiftMultiCell wraps) on the GeoLift_Test panel — cell A = {chicago, portland} (real effect), cell B = {atlanta, boston} (placebo), the rest shared controls: the per-cell ATT matches augsynth to the decimal (A 156.84, B 119.38), the conformal p-values agree (A ≈0.01, B ≈0.8), and the donor-exclusion invariant holds (A never uses B’s markets). Durable case geolift_multicell; unit tests mlsynth/tests/test_multicell.py.

Core API#

class mlsynth.MULTICELLGEOLIFT(config: MultiCellGeoLiftConfig | dict)#

Multi-cell GeoLift: measure several treatment cells at once.

Given a unit-level cell-membership column ("A", "B", … for treated geos; blank / control_label for controls) and a post_col treatment window, each cell is measured against the shared control pool with the fixed-effect Augmented SCM + conformal inference (the other cells are excluded from each cell’s donor pool), and the cells are compared (GeoLift’s non-overlapping-CI winner rule).

Parameters:

config (MultiCellGeoLiftConfig or dict) – See mlsynth.utils.geolift_helpers.multicell.config.MultiCellGeoLiftConfig.

Examples

>>> from mlsynth import MULTICELLGEOLIFT
>>> res = MULTICELLGEOLIFT({"df": panel, "outcome": "Y", "unitid": "location",
...     "time": "date", "cell_column_name": "cell", "post_col": "post"}).fit()
>>> res.cells["A"].effects.att, res.winner
fit() → MultiCellResults#

Resolve the cells, measure each against the shared control, compare.

class mlsynth.utils.geolift_helpers.multicell.config.MultiCellGeoLiftConfig(*, df: DataFrame, outcome: str, unitid: str, time: str, cell_column_name: str, post_col: str, control_label: str | None = None, how: str = 'mean', augment: str | None = 'ridge', fixed_effects: bool = True, alpha: float = 0.1, cpic: float | None = None, ns: int = 1000, conformal_type: str = 'iid', seed: int = 0, display_graphs: bool = True)#

Configuration for the multi-cell GeoLift analysis.

model_config: ClassVar[ConfigDict] = {'arbitrary_types_allowed': True, 'extra': 'forbid'}#

Configuration for the model, should be a dictionary conforming to [ConfigDict][pydantic.config.ConfigDict].
